agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics platform introducing automated route optimization for drivers, integrating delivery assignment, real-time recalculation triggers, customer notifications, and vehicle-class safety constraints. This feature sits at the intersection of several bounded contexts and encodes both hard safety invariants and configurable operational policies.","bounded_context":"This feature spans at least four bounded contexts: (1) Route Planning — owns route calculation, optimization strategy, recalculation triggers, and stop-time estimates; (2) Fleet Management — owns driver profiles, shift lifecycle, vehicle classifications, and zone assignments; (3) Delivery Management — owns delivery lifecycle (pending, assigned, in-progress, failed, completed), zone membership, and parcel attributes (size, weight, vehicle class requirement); (4) Customer Notification — owns notification triggers, delivery ETA communication, and the 'next 3 stops' window concept. Each context should communicate via domain events, not direct coupling.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Optimized Route","definition":"An ordered sequence of delivery stops assigned to a driver, calculated to minimize travel time/distance within their zone, respecting vehicle-class constraints."},{"term":"Zone","definition":"A geographic region used to partition pending deliveries and assign them to drivers whose shift covers that zone."},{"term":"Shift","definition":"A bounded work period during which a driver is active and eligible for route assignment. A shift has a start and end, and is scoped to one or more zones."},{"term":"Recalculation Trigger","definition":"A domain event that invalidates the current optimized route and forces recomputation. Three defined triggers: new delivery added to zone, delivery marked as failed, driver manual deviation."},{"term":"Manual Deviation","definition":"When a driver physically departs from the suggested route sequence (e.g., skips a stop, takes a different path), detected by the system and triggering recalculation."},{"term":"Next 3 Stops Window","definition":"A sliding window of the three upcoming delivery stops on a driver's active route. Entry into this window triggers a customer notification."},{"term":"Vehicle Class","definition":"A classification of a vehicle's capabilities (e.g., motorcycle, van, truck, freight). Determines which deliveries the vehicle is eligible to carry based on parcel requirements."},{"term":"Licensed Vehicle Class","definition":"The vehicle class(es) a driver is authorized and equipped to operate during their shift."},{"term":"Stop Time Estimate","definition":"The expected duration a driver spends at each delivery stop. Defaults to 4 minutes but is overridable per zone by the operations team."},{"term":"Failed Delivery","definition":"A delivery attempt that did not result in successful handoff (e.g., customer not home, access denied), removing it from the active route and triggering recalculation."},{"term":"Pending Delivery","definition":"A delivery that has been created and is awaiting assignment to a driver's route within the appropriate zone."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"When a driver starts their shift, the system must automatically generate and assign an optimized route from all pending deliveries in their zone.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy — the trigger (shift start) and the action (auto-assign) are business decisions that could be changed (e.g., manual assignment mode). The route must be generated, but the automation trigger is policy.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that within N seconds of a driver's shift starting, a route containing all pending deliveries in their zone is assigned to them."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"The route must be recalculated when a new delivery is added to the driver's active zone.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Recalculation policy could be changed to batch updates or manual triggers. It is a business preference for real-time optimization.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that when a new delivery is created in a zone with an active driver route, the route is recalculated to include the new delivery."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"The route must be recalculated when a delivery on the route is marked as failed.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Same as BR-2 — recalculation timing is policy. The removal of the failed delivery from the route sequence is the invariant part (see BR-8).","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that when a delivery is marked as failed, the driver's route is recalculated excluding that delivery."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The route must be recalculated when the driver manually deviates from the suggested route.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Deviation-triggered recalculation is an operational policy. The system could instead alert the driver without recalculating.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that when the system detects the driver has deviated from the prescribed stop order or path, a recalculation is triggered."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Customers must be notified when their delivery enters the 'next 3 stops' window on the driver's active route.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The notification and the window size (3) are business policy, changeable by product decision.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that when a delivery becomes one of the next 3 upcoming stops, a notification is sent to the corresponding customer exactly once for that route calculation."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"A driver must never be assigned a delivery that requires a vehicle class exceeding their licensed vehicle class.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a safety and legal constraint. Assigning freight to a motorcycle driver is physically impossible and potentially illegal. Violating this corrupts system integrity and creates real-world danger.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that for every delivery on a driver's route, delivery.requiredVehicleClass is compatible with driver.licensedVehicleClass. The system must reject any route that violates this."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"The default estimated stop time is 4 minutes per delivery stop, overridable per zone by the operations team.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Both the default value and the override mechanism are operational policies tunable by ops.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that route ETA calculations use the zone-specific override if set, otherwise default to 4 minutes per stop."},{"id":"BR-8","rule":"A failed delivery must be removed from the active route and must not be re-assigned to the same route without explicit intervention.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Continuing to route a driver to a known-failed delivery wastes time and creates a corrupt route state. The delivery has exited the 'assignable' lifecycle state.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that a delivery with status=FAILED never appears in any active route unless explicitly re-queued by dispatch."},{"id":"BR-9","rule":"A driver's route may only contain deliveries within their assigned zone.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Zone boundaries are a fundamental partitioning constraint. Cross-zone assignment breaks the optimization model and operational accountability.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that every delivery on a driver's route belongs to the same zone as the driver's shift assignment."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A driver starts their shift with 10 pending deliveries in their zone","when":"The shift is activated","then":"An optimized route containing all 10 deliveries is automatically assigned to the driver within the system's SLA window"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A driver has an active route with 8 remaining stops","when":"A new delivery is added to their zone","then":"The route is recalculated to include the new delivery, and the driver sees the updated route"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A driver has an active route and delivery #4 is marked as failed","when":"The failure is recorded","then":"The route is recalculated excluding delivery #4, and the stop sequence is re-optimized"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A driver has an active route with stops A→B→C→D","when":"The driver skips stop B and proceeds to stop C","then":"The system detects the deviation and recalculates the route from the driver's current position"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A customer's delivery is currently 4th in the driver's route sequence","when":"The delivery moves into the 'next 3 stops' position (e.g., the driver completes the 1st stop)","then":"The customer receives a notification that their delivery is approaching"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A motorcycle driver is active in a zone","when":"A freight-class delivery is added to that zone","then":"The freight delivery is NOT included in the motorcycle driver's route, even if no other driver is available"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"The operations team has set the stop time for Zone A to 6 minutes","when":"A route is calculated for a driver in Zone A","then":"Each stop's ETA uses 6 minutes, not the default 4 minutes"},{"id":"AC-8","given":"A driver's route is being recalculated due to a new delivery","when":"The driver is currently en route to their next stop","then":"The recalculated route preserves the driver's current next stop and optimizes the remaining sequence"},{"id":"AC-9","given":"A recalculation changes the route such that a previously-notified customer is no longer in the next 3 stops","when":"The recalculation completes","then":"The system handles this gracefully — the customer is not sent a retraction, and re-enters the notification window when they are again within 3 stops"},{"id":"AC-10","given":"Two drivers are active in the same zone","when":"Routes are calculated","then":"Each delivery is assigned to exactly one driver, not duplicated across routes"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Recalculation storm: a burst of new deliveries arrives in rapid succession","expected_behavior":"The system should debounce recalculation triggers to avoid thrashing. Multiple triggers within a short window should be batched into a single recalculation."},{"scenario":"Driver completes all stops but a new delivery arrives before shift ends","expected_behavior":"The system should assign a new single-stop route if the driver's shift is still active and the delivery matches their vehicle class."},{"scenario":"All deliveries in a zone require a vehicle class no active driver can serve","expected_behavior":"Deliveries remain in pending status. The system should alert dispatch/operations that unserviceable deliveries exist in the zone."},{"scenario":"Driver's shift ends mid-route with remaining deliveries","expected_behavior":"Remaining deliveries return to pending status and become eligible for reassignment to the next available driver."},{"scenario":"A delivery is added to a zone with no active drivers","expected_behavior":"The delivery remains pending. No route is created. It will be picked up when the next driver starts a shift in that zone."},{"scenario":"Customer notification sent, then recalculation moves delivery to a different driver","expected_behavior":"The original notification stands. The new driver's route triggers a fresh 'next 3 stops' evaluation. Customer may receive a second notification — acceptable since delivery is still approaching."},{"scenario":"Driver manually deviates and then returns to the original route","expected_behavior":"Deviation detection triggers recalculation regardless. The recalculated route may match the original if it's still optimal."},{"scenario":"Zone override stop time is set to 0 or a negative value","expected_behavior":"The system should reject invalid stop time values. Minimum stop time should be enforced (e.g., 1 minute)."},{"scenario":"A delivery is marked failed and then the customer calls to say they're now available","expected_behavior":"Dispatch must explicitly re-queue the delivery to pending status. It is not automatically re-added to any route."},{"scenario":"Two concurrent recalculation triggers (new delivery + failed delivery at the same instant)","expected_behavior":"The system should process both events and produce a single recalculated route reflecting both changes, not two sequential recalculations."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Route optimization directly reduces fuel costs, increases deliveries per shift, and improves asset utilization. Poor optimization or frequent thrashing could negate these savings. Vehicle-class violations could result in regulatory fines and liability exposure.","operational":"Automatic route assignment reduces dispatcher workload and decision latency. Recalculation triggers keep routes current but introduce computational load. The 4-minute default stop time directly affects capacity planning — incorrect values cascade into missed ETAs and driver overtime.","user":"Customers benefit from 'next 3 stops' visibility, reducing missed-delivery rates. Drivers benefit from not having to plan routes manually. However, frequent recalculations could frustrate drivers with constantly changing instructions.","risk":"Vehicle-class constraint violation is the highest-severity risk — it creates physical safety hazards and legal liability. Notification timing errors are lower severity but damage customer trust. Recalculation race conditions could produce inconsistent route states.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Drivers","impact":"Core workflow change — routes are now auto-assigned and auto-updated. Must trust system optimization. May feel loss of autonomy. Frequent recalculations could be disorienting."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Improved delivery visibility via 'next 3 stops' notification. ETA accuracy depends on stop-time calibration. May receive notifications that become stale after recalculation."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"Gains zone-level stop-time override capability. Must monitor for unserviceable deliveries (vehicle class mismatches with no eligible drivers). Reduced manual dispatch workload."},{"group":"Dispatch","impact":"Less manual route planning, but must handle edge cases (failed deliveries, re-queuing, shift-end handoffs). Needs visibility into recalculation events."},{"group":"Compliance/Legal","impact":"Vehicle-class constraints encode legal requirements. System must produce audit trails proving no violations occurred."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The spec provides clear recalculation triggers and a hard vehicle-class constraint, but several operationally critical details are ambiguous: what 'optimized' means (objective function), how deviation is detected, notification idempotency on recalculation, multi-driver zone assignment strategy, and the interaction between recalculation and in-flight driver navigation. These ambiguities affect implementation correctness significantly.","high_confidence_areas":["Vehicle-class constraint is a hard invariant — unambiguous and safety-critical","Three recalculation triggers are explicitly enumerated","Stop-time default and override mechanism is clearly stated","Zone-scoped delivery assignment is well-defined"],"low_confidence_areas":["Optimization objective function not specified — time vs distance vs priority vs cost","Deviation detection mechanism — GPS-based? Stop-sequence based? Threshold?","Multi-driver zone behavior — how are deliveries partitioned among concurrent drivers?","Notification lifecycle on recalculation — what happens when 'next 3' window shifts?","Shift boundary handling — what happens to in-progress routes when shift ends?","Recalculation concurrency — how to handle overlapping trigger events"],"assumptions":["Vehicle class compatibility is a strict hierarchy or category match, not a fuzzy capability score","Zones are pre-defined geographic regions, not dynamically computed","A driver is assigned to exactly one zone per shift","Route optimization runs server-side, not on the driver's device","Notifications are push-based (not pull-based) to customers"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The optimization objective is unspecified. 'Optimized route' could mean shortest distance, shortest time, fewest left turns, priority-weighted, or balanced workload across drivers. The choice fundamentally changes route output and customer ETAs.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Customers","Operations Team"],"options":["Optimize for shortest total travel time","Optimize for shortest total distance","Optimize for delivery priority (SLA deadlines first)","Multi-objective optimization with configurable weights"],"recommendation":"Define a primary objective (likely shortest travel time) with secondary constraints (priority deliveries first, balanced driver workload). Make this configurable per zone by operations."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Manual deviation detection mechanism is undefined. It is unclear whether deviation means the driver visited stops out of order, took a different road, or moved beyond a GPS radius from the planned path. Each interpretation has different detection complexity and false-positive rates.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Operations Team"],"options":["Stop-sequence deviation: driver skips or reorders a stop","GPS path deviation: driver is more than X meters from planned path","Time deviation: driver hasn't arrived at expected stop within threshold"],"recommendation":"Use stop-sequence deviation as the primary trigger (driver completes a stop that wasn't next in sequence, or skips a stop). GPS path deviation is noisy and infrastructure-heavy. Stop-sequence is deterministic and business-meaningful."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Multi-driver zone assignment strategy is unspecified. When multiple drivers are active in the same zone, the spec doesn't clarify how pending deliveries are partitioned — nearest-neighbor, balanced count, balanced workload, or driver preference.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Customers","Dispatch"],"options":["Assign all deliveries to a single driver, overflow to next","Partition by geographic sub-clusters within the zone","Balance by estimated total route time across drivers","Balance by delivery count across drivers"],"recommendation":"Partition by geographic clustering within the zone, then balance by estimated route time. This minimizes cross-zone travel while keeping workloads fair."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Customer notification behavior during recalculation is unclear. When a recalculation moves a delivery out of the 'next 3 stops' window after a notification was already sent, the spec doesn't define whether to retract, ignore, or re-notify.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers"],"options":["Send notification once per delivery per route — never retract","Retract notification if delivery leaves the 3-stop window","Send update notification with revised ETA"],"recommendation":"Send notification once when delivery first enters the 3-stop window. Do not retract. If recalculation moves it out and then back in, do not re-notify. One notification per delivery per shift."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics platform introducing automated route optimization for drivers, integrating delivery assignment, real-time recalculation triggers, customer notifications, and vehicle-class safety constraints. This feature sits at the intersection of several bounded contexts and encodes both hard safety invariants and configurable operational policies.","bounded_context":"This feature spans at least four bounded contexts: (1) Route Planning — owns route calculat

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this logistics domain scenario carefully.

The feature "Smart Route Optimization" spans multiple bounded contexts. Let me identify them and the business rules.

Bounded contexts I see:
1. Route Planning / Optimization - route calculation, recalculation triggers
2. Fleet/Driver Management - vehicle class licensing, shift management
3. Delivery Management - delivery lifecycle, zones
4. Customer Notification - next 3 stops window

Business rules:
1. Auto-assign optimized route on shift start


2. Recalculate when a new delivery arrives in the zone
3. Recalculate after a failed delivery attempt
4. Recalculate if the driver manually deviates from the route
5. Notify customers when they're in the next 3 stops
6. Enforce vehicle class constraints—motorcycles can't handle freight
7. Default to 4 minutes per stop, but allow zone-specific overrides

Now I'm thinking through the ambiguities in the spec. There are some gaps around what happens mid-recalculation, how zones are actually defined and assigned to drivers, what vehicle classes exist beyond the motorcycle example, and how the system detects when a driver has manually deviated from their assigned route.

I'm also uncertain about the customer notification behavior—whether they get notified once or receive updates, what happens if recalculation shifts who falls into that next 3 stops window, and what "optimized" actually means in the context of route planning.

Make this configurable per zone by operations."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Manual deviation detection mechanism is undefined. It is unclear whether deviation means the driver visited stops out of order, took a different road, or moved beyond a GPS radius from the planned path. Each interpretation has different detection complexity and false-positive rates.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Operations Team"],"options":["Stop-sequence deviation: driver skips or reorders a stop","GPS path deviation: driver is more than X meters from planned path","Time deviation: driver hasn't arrived at expected stop within threshold"],"recommendation":"Use stop-sequence deviation as the primary trigger (driver completes a stop that wasn't next in sequence, or skips a stop). GPS path deviation is noisy and infrastructure-heavy. Stop-sequence is deterministic and business-meaningful."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Multi-driver zone assignment strategy is unspecified. When multiple drivers are active in the same zone, the spec doesn't clarify how pending deliveries are partitioned — nearest-neighbor, balanced count, balanced workload, or driver preference.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Customers","Dispatch"],"options":["Assign all deliveries to a single driver, overflow to next","Partition by geographic sub-clusters within the zone","Balance by estimated total route time across drivers","Balance by delivery count across drivers"],"recommendation":"Partition by geographic clustering within the zone, then balance by estimated route time. This minimizes cross-zone travel while keeping workloads fair."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Customer notification behavior during recalculation is unclear. When a recalculation moves a delivery out of the 'next 3 stops' window after a notification was already sent, the spec doesn't define whether to retract, ignore, or re-notify.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers"],"options":["Send notification once per delivery per route — never retract","Retract notification if delivery leaves the 3-stop window","Send update notification with revised ETA"],"recommendation":"Send notification once when delivery first enters the 3-stop window. Do not retract. If recalculation moves it out and then back in, do not re-notify. One notification per delivery per shift."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}

trace.result: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics platform introducing automated route optimization for drivers, integrating delivery assignment, real-time recalculation triggers, customer notifications, and vehicle-class safety constraints. This feature sits at the intersection of several bounded contexts and encodes both hard safety invariants and configurable operational policies.","bounded_context":"This feature spans at least four bounded contexts: (1) Route Planning — owns route calculation, optimization strategy, recalculation triggers, and stop-time estimates; (2) Fleet Management — owns driver profiles, shift lifecycle, vehicle classifications, and zone assignments; (3) Delivery Management — owns delivery lifecycle (pending, assigned, in-progress, failed, completed), zone membership, and parcel attributes (size, weight, vehicle class requirement); (4) Customer Notification — owns notification triggers, delivery ETA communication, and the 'next 3 stops' window concept. Each context should communicate via domain events, not direct coupling.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Optimized Route","definition":"An ordered sequence of delivery stops assigned to a driver, calculated to minimize travel time/distance within their zone, respecting vehicle-class constraints."},{"term":"Zone","definition":"A geographic region used to partition pending deliveries and assign them to drivers whose shift covers that zone."},{"term":"Shift","definition":"A bounded work period during which a driver is active and eligible for route assignment. A shift has a start and end, and is scoped to one or more zones."},{"term":"Recalculation Trigger","definition":"A domain event that invalidates the current optimized route and forces recomputation. Three defined triggers: new delivery added to zone, delivery marked as failed, driver manual deviation."},{"term":"Manual Deviation","definition":"When a driver physically departs from the suggested route sequence (e.g., skips a stop, takes a different path), detected by the system and triggering recalculation."},{"term":"Next 3 Stops Window","definition":"A sliding window of the three upcoming delivery stops on a driver's active route. Entry into this window triggers a customer notification."},{"term":"Vehicle Class","definition":"A classification of a vehicle's capabilities (e.g., motorcycle, van, truck, freight). Determines which deliveries the vehicle is eligible to carry based on parcel requirements."},{"term":"Licensed Vehicle Class","definition":"The vehicle class(es) a driver is authorized and equipped to operate during their shift."},{"term":"Stop Time Estimate","definition":"The expected duration a driver spends at each delivery stop. Defaults to 4 minutes but is overridable per zone by the operations team."},{"term":"Failed Delivery","definition":"A delivery attempt that did not result in successful handoff (e.g., customer not home, access denied), removing it from the active route and triggering recalculation."},{"term":"Pending Delivery","definition":"A delivery that has been created and is awaiting assignment to a driver's route within the appropriate zone."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"When a driver starts their shift, the system must automatically generate and assign an optimized route from all pending deliveries in their zone.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy — the trigger (shift start) and the action (auto-assign) are business decisions that could be changed (e.g., manual assignment mode). The route must be generated, but the automation trigger is policy.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that within N seconds of a driver's shift starting, a route containing all pending deliveries in their zone is assigned to them."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"The route must be recalculated when a new delivery is added to the driver's active zone.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Recalculation policy could be changed to batch updates or manual triggers. It is a business preference for real-time optimization.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that when a new delivery is created in a zone with an active driver route, the route is recalculated to include the new delivery."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"The route must be recalculated when a delivery on the route is marked as failed.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Same as BR-2 — recalculation timing is policy. The removal of the failed delivery from the route sequence is the invariant part (see BR-8).","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that when a delivery is marked as failed, the driver's route is recalculated excluding that delivery."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The route must be recalculated when the driver manually deviates from the suggested route.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Deviation-triggered recalculation is an operational policy. The system could instead alert the driver without recalculating.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that when the system detects the driver has deviated from the prescribed stop order or path, a recalculation is triggered."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Customers must be notified when their delivery enters the 'next 3 stops' window on the driver's active route.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The notification and the window size (3) are business policy, changeable by product decision.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that when a delivery becomes one of the next 3 upcoming stops, a notification is sent to the corresponding customer exactly once for that route calculation."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"A driver must never be assigned a delivery that requires a vehicle class exceeding their licensed vehicle class.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a safety and legal constraint. Assigning freight to a motorcycle driver is physically impossible and potentially illegal. Violating this corrupts system integrity and creates real-world danger.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that for every delivery on a driver's route, delivery.requiredVehicleClass is compatible with driver.licensedVehicleClass. The system must reject any route that violates this."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"The default estimated stop time is 4 minutes per delivery stop, overridable per zone by the operations team.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Both the default value and the override mechanism are operational policies tunable by ops.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that route ETA calculations use the zone-specific override if set, otherwise default to 4 minutes per stop."},{"id":"BR-8","rule":"A failed delivery must be removed from the active route and must not be re-assigned to the same route without explicit intervention.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Continuing to route a driver to a known-failed delivery wastes time and creates a corrupt route state. The delivery has exited the 'assignable' lifecycle state.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that a delivery with status=FAILED never appears in any active route unless explicitly re-queued by dispatch."},{"id":"BR-9","rule":"A driver's route may only contain deliveries within their assigned zone.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Zone boundaries are a fundamental partitioning constraint. Cross-zone assignment breaks the optimization model and operational accountability.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that every delivery on a driver's route belongs to the same zone as the driver's shift assignment."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A driver starts their shift with 10 pending deliveries in their zone","when":"The shift is activated","then":"An optimized route containing all 10 deliveries is automatically assigned to the driver within the system's SLA window"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A driver has an active route with 8 remaining stops","when":"A new delivery is added to their zone","then":"The route is recalculated to include the new delivery, and the driver sees the updated route"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A driver has an active route and delivery #4 is marked as failed","when":"The failure is recorded","then":"The route is recalculated excluding delivery #4, and the stop sequence is re-optimized"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A driver has an active route with stops A→B→C→D","when":"The driver skips stop B and proceeds to stop C","then":"The system detects the deviation and recalculates the route from the driver's current position"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A customer's delivery is currently 4th in the driver's route sequence","when":"The delivery moves into the 'next 3 stops' position (e.g., the driver completes the 1st stop)","then":"The customer receives a notification that their delivery is approaching"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A motorcycle driver is active in a zone","when":"A freight-class delivery is added to that zone","then":"The freight delivery is NOT included in the motorcycle driver's route, even if no other driver is available"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"The operations team has set the stop time for Zone A to 6 minutes","when":"A route is calculated for a driver in Zone A","then":"Each stop's ETA uses 6 minutes, not the default 4 minutes"},{"id":"AC-8","given":"A driver's route is being recalculated due to a new delivery","when":"The driver is currently en route to their next stop","then":"The recalculated route preserves the driver's current next stop and optimizes the remaining sequence"},{"id":"AC-9","given":"A recalculation changes the route such that a previously-notified customer is no longer in the next 3 stops","when":"The recalculation completes","then":"The system handles this gracefully — the customer is not sent a retraction, and re-enters the notification window when they are again within 3 stops"},{"id":"AC-10","given":"Two drivers are active in the same zone","when":"Routes are calculated","then":"Each delivery is assigned to exactly one driver, not duplicated across routes"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Recalculation storm: a burst of new deliveries arrives in rapid succession","expected_behavior":"The system should debounce recalculation triggers to avoid thrashing. Multiple triggers within a short window should be batched into a single recalculation."},{"scenario":"Driver completes all stops but a new delivery arrives before shift ends","expected_behavior":"The system should assign a new single-stop route if the driver's shift is still active and the delivery matches their vehicle class."},{"scenario":"All deliveries in a zone require a vehicle class no active driver can serve","expected_behavior":"Deliveries remain in pending status. The system should alert dispatch/operations that unserviceable deliveries exist in the zone."},{"scenario":"Driver's shift ends mid-route with remaining deliveries","expected_behavior":"Remaining deliveries return to pending status and become eligible for reassignment to the next available driver."},{"scenario":"A delivery is added to a zone with no active drivers","expected_behavior":"The delivery remains pending. No route is created. It will be picked up when the next driver starts a shift in that zone."},{"scenario":"Customer notification sent, then recalculation moves delivery to a different driver","expected_behavior":"The original notification stands. The new driver's route triggers a fresh 'next 3 stops' evaluation. Customer may receive a second notification — acceptable since delivery is still approaching."},{"scenario":"Driver manually deviates and then returns to the original route","expected_behavior":"Deviation detection triggers recalculation regardless. The recalculated route may match the original if it's still optimal."},{"scenario":"Zone override stop time is set to 0 or a negative value","expected_behavior":"The system should reject invalid stop time values. Minimum stop time should be enforced (e.g., 1 minute)."},{"scenario":"A delivery is marked failed and then the customer calls to say they're now available","expected_behavior":"Dispatch must explicitly re-queue the delivery to pending status. It is not automatically re-added to any route."},{"scenario":"Two concurrent recalculation triggers (new delivery + failed delivery at the same instant)","expected_behavior":"The system should process both events and produce a single recalculated route reflecting both changes, not two sequential recalculations."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Route optimization directly reduces fuel costs, increases deliveries per shift, and improves asset utilization. Poor optimization or frequent thrashing could negate these savings. Vehicle-class violations could result in regulatory fines and liability exposure.","operational":"Automatic route assignment reduces dispatcher workload and decision latency. Recalculation triggers keep routes current but introduce computational load. The 4-minute default stop time directly affects capacity planning — incorrect values cascade into missed ETAs and driver overtime.","user":"Customers benefit from 'next 3 stops' visibility, reducing missed-delivery rates. Drivers benefit from not having to plan routes manually. However, frequent recalculations could frustrate drivers with constantly changing instructions.","risk":"Vehicle-class constraint violation is the highest-severity risk — it creates physical safety hazards and legal liability. Notification timing errors are lower severity but damage customer trust. Recalculation race conditions could produce inconsistent route states.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Drivers","impact":"Core workflow change — routes are now auto-assigned and auto-updated. Must trust system optimization. May feel loss of autonomy. Frequent recalculations could be disorienting."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Improved delivery visibility via 'next 3 stops' notification. ETA accuracy depends on stop-time calibration. May receive notifications that become stale after recalculation."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"Gains zone-level stop-time override capability. Must monitor for unserviceable deliveries (vehicle class mismatches with no eligible drivers). Reduced manual dispatch workload."},{"group":"Dispatch","impact":"Less manual route planning, but must handle edge cases (failed deliveries, re-queuing, shift-end handoffs). Needs visibility into recalculation events."},{"group":"Compliance/Legal","impact":"Vehicle-class constraints encode legal requirements. System must produce audit trails proving no violations occurred."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The spec provides clear recalculation triggers and a hard vehicle-class constraint, but several operationally critical details are ambiguous: what 'optimized' means (objective function), how deviation is detected, notification idempotency on recalculation, multi-driver zone assignment strategy, and the interaction between recalculation and in-flight driver navigation. These ambiguities affect implementation correctness significantly.","high_confidence_areas":["Vehicle-class constraint is a hard invariant — unambiguous and safety-critical","Three recalculation triggers are explicitly enumerated","Stop-time default and override mechanism is clearly stated","Zone-scoped delivery assignment is well-defined"],"low_confidence_areas":["Optimization objective function not specified — time vs distance vs priority vs cost","Deviation detection mechanism — GPS-based? Stop-sequence based? Threshold?","Multi-driver zone behavior — how are deliveries partitioned among concurrent drivers?","Notification lifecycle on recalculation — what happens when 'next 3' window shifts?","Shift boundary handling — what happens to in-progress routes when shift ends?","Recalculation concurrency — how to handle overlapping trigger events"],"assumptions":["Vehicle class compatibility is a strict hierarchy or category match, not a fuzzy capability score","Zones are pre-defined geographic regions, not dynamically computed","A driver is assigned to exactly one zone per shift","Route optimization runs server-side, not on the driver's device","Notifications are push-based (not pull-based) to customers"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The optimization objective is unspecified. 'Optimized route' could mean shortest distance, shortest time, fewest left turns, priority-weighted, or balanced workload across drivers. The choice fundamentally changes route output and customer ETAs.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Customers","Operations Team"],"options":["Optimize for shortest total travel time","Optimize for shortest total distance","Optimize for delivery priority (SLA deadlines first)","Multi-objective optimization with configurable weights"],"recommendation":"Define a primary objective (likely shortest travel time) with secondary constraints (priority deliveries first, balanced driver workload). Make this configurable per zone by operations."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Manual deviation detection mechanism is undefined. It is unclear whether deviation means the driver visited stops out of order, took a different road, or moved beyond a GPS radius from the planned path. Each interpretation has different detection complexity and false-positive rates.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Operations Team"],"options":["Stop-sequence deviation: driver skips or reorders a stop","GPS path deviation: driver is more than X meters from planned path","Time deviation: driver hasn't arrived at expected stop within threshold"],"recommendation":"Use stop-sequence deviation as the primary trigger (driver completes a stop that wasn't next in sequence, or skips a stop). GPS path deviation is noisy and infrastructure-heavy. Stop-sequence is deterministic and business-meaningful."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Multi-driver zone assignment strategy is unspecified. When multiple drivers are active in the same zone, the spec doesn't clarify how pending deliveries are partitioned — nearest-neighbor, balanced count, balanced workload, or driver preference.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Customers","Dispatch"],"options":["Assign all deliveries to a single driver, overflow to next","Partition by geographic sub-clusters within the zone","Balance by estimated total route time across drivers","Balance by delivery count across drivers"],"recommendation":"Partition by geographic clustering within the zone, then balance by estimated route time. This minimizes cross-zone travel while keeping workloads fair."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Customer notification behavior during recalculation is unclear. When a recalculation moves a delivery out of the 'next 3 stops' window after a notification was already sent, the spec doesn't define whether to retract, ignore, or re-notify.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers"],"options":["Send notification once per delivery per route — never retract","Retract notification if delivery leaves the 3-stop window","Send update notification with revised ETA"],"recommendation":"Send notification once when delivery first enters the 3-stop window. Do not retract. If recalculation moves it out and then back in, do not re-notify. One notification per delivery per shift."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
